comments.text: A trivial remark: It suffices to show the existence of one solution for each $k$.

I've investigated a little whether the method in [728] can help here as well. It seems that it does allow you to handle up to moderately large primes, specifically if $n+1,\dots,n+k$ are $\exp(c \sqrt{\log n})$-smooth for some $c > 0$ it may work. Unfortunately, current techniques can only give consecutive integers that are roughly $n^\varepsilon$-smooth. Heuristically, one does expect the "$\exp(c \sqrt{\log n})$-smooth" to be true, but this seems far beyond current techniques. So probably [728] isn't helping all that much.
other OEIS sequence: A389396 (for k=2) (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Another OEIS sequence that is definitely interesting to mention here as well, is A343507. In particular, these may lead to the suspicion that the answer is "yes" for every $k \ge 2.$ For $k=2$, there were $913$ such values of $n$ bounded by $10^5.$
Indeed there is a simple heuristic argument (see the comment by Will Sawin on my answer here) that says that for any fixed $k$ there is a positive proportion of such $n$.

The $k=2$ case is tantalizingly close to being within reach of current technology (in particular, the two-point logarithmically averaged Elliott and Chowla technology), which can provide a large number of $n$ with $n+1,n+2$ both somewhat smooth, which increases the odds of $(n+1)^2(n+2)^2$ dividing $\binom{2n}{n}$. Unfortunately the error terms in the current estimates are still somewhat weak (particularly in view of the relatively sparse nature of smooth numbers), and may not be enough to close this problem without additional effort.
